title: Phishing campaign targeting employees
description: Microsoft Defender for Office 365 detected a high-confidence phishing campaign delivered to 47 employee mailboxes over a 20-minute window. The emails impersonated the company's IT helpdesk (sender: it-helpdesk@company-support.net) with subject line 'Urgent: Your VPN access expires in 24 hours — verify now'. The embedded link redirected through two URL shorteners to a credential harvesting page mimicking the company Okta login portal. Defender's Zero-hour Auto Purge (ZAP) automatically quarantined 39 of the 47 delivery attempts before user interaction. Eight emails were read before ZAP acted; two users clicked the link but neither entered credentials, as confirmed by Okta authentication logs showing no new sessions from unusual IPs.
detection_method: Microsoft Defender for Office 365 — ZAP (Zero-hour Auto Purge) with high-confidence phishing verdict; manual user report via PhishAlarm

timeline.event: Microsoft Defender for O365 ZAP fired on phishing campaign: 39/47 emails auto-quarantined with verdict 'High Confidence Phishing'; 8 emails already delivered to read inboxes; user a.griffin@company.com submitted PhishAlarm report simultaneously

On-call security analyst b.osei@company.com acknowledged alert; reviewed Defender Threat Explorer; identified campaign sender domain company-support.net (registered 3 days prior); remaining 8 delivered emails manually purged via admin portal

Okta authentication logs confirmed: 2 users clicked link (c.huang@company.com, d.reyes@company.com) but neither submitted credentials; no new Okta sessions from external IPs matching the harvesting domain; credential compromise ruled out

Harvesting domain company-support.net and all associated URLs blocked at email gateway, proxy, and DNS layer; IOCs (sender domain, IP, URL patterns) submitted to Microsoft Defender TI for blocklist propagation
All-staff advisory sent via Slack #security-alerts; individual follow-up sent to c.huang and d.reyes for credential verification; incident closed with zero credential compromise confirmed

resolution_summary: Microsoft Defender for O365 ZAP auto-quarantined 39 of 47 phishing emails before user interaction. The remaining 8 were manually purged within 2 minutes of acknowledgment. Two employees clicked the credential harvesting link but neither submitted their credentials; Okta logs confirmed no unauthorized authentication sessions from external IPs. The phishing campaign sender domain (company-support.net, 3 days old) and all associated infrastructure were blocked at email gateway, web proxy, and corporate DNS. No credentials were compromised.
root_cause_analysis: The phishing campaign was a credential harvesting operation targeting company employees using a lookalike domain (company-support.net) registered 3 days before the attack. The emails passed initial SPF and DKIM checks because the sender domain was newly registered with valid records — Defender's ML model assigned a high-confidence phishing verdict based on content analysis (urgency language, URL shortener chains, lookalike brand elements) rather than domain reputation alone. Eight emails reached user inboxes during the 3-minute window between first delivery and ZAP quarantine action, a known gap in ZAP's retroactive purge timing. The harvesting page used a pixel-perfect clone of the company Okta portal with a valid Let's Encrypt certificate, adding false legitimacy.
business_impact: Moderate security risk, limited exposure, potential for escalation if not addressed
remediation_actions: Manually purged all 8 remaining delivered phishing emails using Microsoft Defender admin portal Search & Purge: New-ComplianceSearchAction -SearchName 'Phishing-1126' -Purge -PurgeType SoftDelete
Verified Okta auth logs for c.huang@company.com and d.reyes@company.com: confirmed 0 external login attempts from IPs associated with harvesting domain; no session tokens issued
Blocked sender domain company-support.net and 3 associated sending IPs at email gateway (Proofpoint); added to corporate DNS RPZ blocklist and Zscaler URL category 'Phishing'

Extracted full phishing IOCs (sender domain, URL shortener chains, harvesting page IP 185.220.101.x, SHA256 of HTML clone) and submitted to Microsoft Defender Threat Intelligence for global blocklist propagation
Sent targeted security advisory to c.huang and d.reyes: confirmed no credential entry, recommended Okta session audit, offered MFA reset if desired — both declined, sessions confirmed clean
Broadcast all-staff Slack message to #security-alerts with phishing screenshot, indicators, and 'how to report' instructions within 35 minutes of detection
preventive_measures: Microsoft Defender for O365 'First Contact Safety Tip' enabled: banner now displayed on all emails from senders not previously contacted, increasing user suspicion of impersonation emails
Proofpoint email gateway updated to enforce DMARC reject policy for all inbound mail — the phishing sender passed SPF/DKIM but would be blocked under strict DMARC
Zscaler SSL inspection enabled for credential submission events: any form POST to a domain registered within 30 days is blocked and logged
Phishing simulation frequency increased to bi-weekly for all employees; click-rate reporting shared with department managers to drive awareness accountability
IT helpdesk communications policy updated: all IT-initiated email actions now require a parallel in-app banner in the company Okta portal to prevent lookalike domain impersonation
security_controls_updated: Microsoft Defender for O365 Safe Links policy updated: URL shortener services now always unwrapped and scanned before delivery; detonation sandbox timeout extended to 60 seconds
Proofpoint DMARC quarantine policy set to reject for all external senders impersonating company.com domain
Corporate DNS RPZ (Response Policy Zone) updated with company-support.net and 47 lookalike domain variants pre-registered as blocked
Okta Adaptive MFA policy updated: any login from a new device/IP now requires push notification approval regardless of previous session history
documentation_updated: Phishing Incident Response Runbook updated with ZAP purge procedure, Okta session validation query, and proxy blocklist update steps
Employee Security Awareness Guide updated: new section 'IT will never ask for your Okta password via email' with screenshot examples of legitimate vs phishing IT communications
Email Gateway Runbook updated with DMARC reject policy enforcement procedure and lookalike domain detection playbook
PhishAlarm usage statistics for Q4 2025 compiled: 6 campaigns detected, employee report rate 34% — highest quarterly rate to date
lessons_learned: The 3-minute window between ZAP detection and retroactive purge action is an inherent architectural gap in Defender for O365's deferred scanning model — pre-delivery sandboxing (Safe Attachments detonation) needs to be extended to URL content at delivery time, not retroactively. The PhishAlarm user report from a.griffin@company.com arrived simultaneously with the automated ZAP detection, validating the effectiveness of user reporting training. The fastest path to zero compromise in phishing scenarios remains Okta FIDO2/WebAuthn — phishing-resistant MFA would make credential harvesting page submissions useless even if users completed the form.
follow_up_tasks: PH-1126-01: Enable Defender for O365 pre-delivery URL detonation for all emails from first-time senders — Owner: Email Security, Due: 2025-12-05
PH-1126-02: Pilot Okta FIDO2 (hardware key) MFA for executive and finance teams (highest phishing risk) — Owner: IAM, Due: 2026-01-15
PH-1126-03: Pre-register 200 common typosquat/lookalike domains as DNS blocklist entries using dnstwist analysis — Owner: Threat Intel, Due: 2025-12-10
PH-1126-04: Increase phishing simulation frequency to bi-weekly; share department-level click rate report with VP of Engineering — Owner: Security Awareness, Due: 2025-12-01
resolution: Zero credentials compromised; all 47 phishing emails quarantined or purged; sender infrastructure fully blocked at email, proxy, and DNS layers
action_taken: Resolved by security-team within 35 minutes of Microsoft Defender ZAP detection
detection_gap: Microsoft Defender ZAP (Zero-hour Auto Purge) operates retroactively after initial delivery — phishing emails are accessible to recipients for several minutes before ZAP quarantine executes; the phishing URL was not yet blocklisted during initial SMTP delivery, bypassing URL reputation filters at send time
what_went_well: Defender ZAP retroactively quarantined the phishing email and removed the credential harvesting URL from all recipient mailboxes before any user clicked the embedded link
No credential compromise was observed — the recipient who opened the email did not interact with the embedded link
Company-wide notification was issued within 2 hours of detection, enabling proactive awareness across all employees before any follow-up phishing wave
what_went_wrong: ZAP's retroactive quarantine model created a delivery window where the phishing email was accessible to recipients — synchronous pre-delivery blocking was not available for this campaign
Email gateway did not flag the phishing attempt at initial SMTP delivery because the malicious URL domain had no negative reputation at send time
No company-wide phishing template awareness training had been conducted in the prior 6 months, reducing employee recognition of this campaign style
